topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the backyard felt strangely thin that afternoon. Elias walked toward the old oak, the one he’d painstakingly nurtured into a towering sentinel when he was ten. He stopped dead. Where the massive trunk should have stood, there was only a jagged scar on the earth, freshly churned and bare. His breath hitched, a shallow, quick thing in his chest. He knelt down, his fingers brushing against the disturbed soil, the memory of tiny saplings and hopeful watering cans flooding back, now tainted by a cold, heavy dread. He stood up slowly, his gaze sweeping the perimeter of the yard, searching for any sign of a machine, any trace of the work. Nothing. Just the exposed roots, pale and vulnerable against the sun.

[story 2]

"That's impossible," Sarah whispered, the sound barely audible over the distant hum of traffic. She stared at the stump, a grotesque monument to a vanished hope. Her hands clenched into fists at her sides, the knuckles turning white. She circled the area three times, her footsteps crunching on dry leaves, each step feeling clumsy and wrong. A tremor ran through her legs, making her sway slightly. She leaned against the fence, closing her eyes tightly, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of her heart. When she opened them, the silence of the empty space around the former tree seemed to press in on her, suffocating and absolute.

[story 3]

Mark approached the clearing with deliberate caution. He didn't rush; every movement was measured, almost hesitant. When he finally saw it—the cleared patch where his childhood tree had anchored itself—a profound stillness settled over him. He felt a strange tightness in his throat, a sudden inability to draw a full breath. He lowered his gaze to the ground, tracing the outline of the cut, his fingers trembling slightly as they hovered near the raw wood. A low, involuntary sound escaped him, a choked exhale that seemed too loud in the quiet afternoon. He took a step back, his eyes wide, as if expecting the ground to shift beneath him.
